method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is part of a large, mostly submerged volcano situated along the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the Pacific Ocean, which results from the subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcano rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extends about 20 km across the ocean bottom, with its summit occupied by a large caldera roughly 5 km in diameter.[4] Only two small portions of the caldera rim emerged above sea level to form the uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga, to the north, and Hunga Haʻapai, to the west.[2][4]

The two islands were first described in detail by Bryan et al. (1972), who noted they were "elongated and tangent to a circle centered on a rocky shoal about 3 km to the south of Hunga Tonga, which was the site of volcanic eruptions in 1912 and 1937".[2] Samples collected on Hunga Tonga consisted of alternating layers of andesitic lava flows and beds of scoria, lapilli, and ash, which dipped gently away from the center of the circle, leading the authors to hypothesize that the islands were the visible northern and western remnants of the ridge around a large submarine caldera atop an active volcano.[2] The andesitic composition of Hunga's eruptive products is typical for intra-oceanic arcs.[4]

High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys later confirmed this hypothesis, revealing a 4 × 2 kilometer, 150 meter deep depression at the location of the submarine caldera anticipated by Bryan et al. (1972).[2] A feature of this size would have formed by the explosive collapse of a previous volcanic structure during an earlier eruption, and past caldera-forming events at Hunga are estimated to have caused global cooling of about 1°C.[2]

Following the 2014–2015 eruption, a new volcanic cone of tephra joined the two previously separate islands into a single landmass, which was named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] At that time the combined island covered just over 1 km² with a maximum altitude of 149 m.[5] The unified island remained largely intact, with only minor shoreline erosion and settling, through November 2021.[2] After the catastrophic main eruption of 15 January 2022, the island was almost entirely destroyed, with only very small fragments remaining at the western and eastern edges, corresponding to remnants of the former Hunga Haʻapai and Hunga Tonga islands.[4][5]

==Geography==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is located in the South Pacific Ocean within the Kingdom of Tonga, situated approximately 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga's main and most populous island, and about 30 km south of the submarine volcano of Fonuafoʻou.[4] The volcano lies along the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, a vast subduction system extending roughly 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[5]

Prior to the 2014–2015 eruption, the site consisted of two separate uninhabited islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, which represented the only emergent portions of the otherwise submerged caldera rim.[5] The undersea eruptive activity between December 2014 and January 2015 produced a new volcanic cone, with accumulated tephra bridging the two pre-existing islands to form a single, conjoined landmass.[1] This newly formed island became notable as one of the first landforms of its kind to persist for several years in the satellite era, drawing scientific interest in studying its morphological evolution and erosion processes, which have also been proposed as useful analogues for interpreting similar features on Mars.[5]

The wider island group sits within a region of dense human settlement on nearby islands, with roughly 75% of Tonga's population residing on Tongatapu.[4] The surrounding Haʻapai, Tongatapu and ʻEua island groups, together with coastlines across the broader Pacific, lie within the zone of influence of the volcano's eruptive activity.[4]

==Eruptions==
## Historical activity and 2014–2015 eruption

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai has experienced repeated eruptive episodes along the Tonga–Kermadec arc, with its summit caldera shaped by past explosive collapses. The island visible above water formed the rim of a submarine caldera approximately 5 km in diameter, itself the uppermost portion of a large underwater volcano rising 1.8 km from the seafloor and spanning roughly 20 km across.[3]

## 15 January 2022 eruption

On 15 January 2022, at about 5 p.m. local time, the undersea volcano began a violent eruption, producing massive explosions and sending gas and ash approximately 36 miles (58 km) into the atmosphere.[4] Satellite observations recorded a vertical plume more than 30 km tall with overshooting tops above 55 km, a record in the satellite era.[5] Estimates based on surface pressure data placed the single-event energy release from the initial explosion between 10 and 28 exajoules, probably larger than the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption (around 10 EJ) and possibly comparable to Krakatoa in 1883 (around 30 EJ).[5]

The eruption was unusual among Surtseyan-style events because of the enormous volume of seawater that interacted with the magma. In a typical Surtseyan eruption, only a small amount of water contacts the magma, producing brief flashes of steam, but on 15 January something weakened the hard rock of the volcano's foundation and caused a partial collapse of the caldera's northern rim, allowing huge amounts of water to rush into an underground magma chamber where temperatures exceeded 1,000 °C.[3] Because magma temperatures are roughly 1,000 °C while seawater is near 20 °C, the mixing of the two in the confined space of the magma chamber was exceptionally explosive, leading some volcanologists to propose a new "ultra Surtseyan" designation for this style of event.[3] The eruption occurred from a vent approximately 200 meters below the ocean's surface.[1]

## Immediate impacts

Tsunamis triggered by the eruption swept onto Tongan islands, reaching heights of up to 65 feet (20 m) above sea level, destroying hundreds of structures, displacing more than 1,500 people, and causing four deaths.[4] Tsunami waves struck shores across the Pacific, leading to two deaths and an oil spill in Peru, as well as damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] The eruption also severed Tonga's domestic internet cable, which was later found buried beneath 100 feet of sediment and ash.[4] Submarine surveys subsequently observed fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples as far as 50 km from the volcano, along with gouged valleys and enormous piles of sediment, recording up to 1.67 cubic miles of displaced material—equivalent to roughly 3 million Olympic-sized swimming pools.[4]

The eruption almost entirely destroyed the landmass that had united the two islands since 2014–2015, leaving only small remnants of the original Hunga Haʻapai and Hunga Tonga at the western and eastern edges of the caldera rim.[3]

## Monitoring challenges

Because no seismometers were installed on Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai, the nearest functioning seismic station at the time of the January 2022 eruption was nearly 500 miles away, leaving scientists without the kind of local earthquake data typically used to track pre-eruptive magma movement.[4] To address this gap, a team from the U.S. Geological Survey's National Earthquake Information Center (NEIC) worked with the USGS Volcano Disaster Assistance Program (VDAP) to develop methods for detecting earthquakes that NEIC's automated system had missed, constructing a near-real-time catalog of seismic activity near the volcano in the days and weeks following the eruption.[4]

## Atmospheric and climatic effects

The 2022 eruption injected vast amounts of volcanic aerosols and water vapor into the atmosphere over a remarkable two-day period.[2] As a submarine volcano erupting from a vent only 200 meters below the sea surface, it introduced an unprecedented quantity of water vapor into the stratosphere, increasing total stratospheric water content by about 10%, while also releasing a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide that was rapidly converted into sulfate aerosols.[2][1] Because sulfate aerosols reflect sunlight and cool the atmosphere while stratospheric water vapor tends to warm it, scientists were initially uncertain which effect would dominate, and some expected the eruption might push global temperatures past the 1.5 °C threshold set by the Paris Agreement.[1]

A UCLA-led study found that the eruption did not warm the planet but instead reduced temperatures over the Southern Hemisphere by about 0.1 °C, largely due to sunlight-reflecting aerosol particles, while a very slight warming influence was detected in the Northern Hemisphere because water vapor can linger for years in the stratosphere.[1] Overall, the 2022 Hunga eruption produced a slight net cooling effect on the planet, demonstrating that shallow undersea eruptions can trigger complex atmospheric changes distinct from those of large terrestrial eruptions such as Tambora in 1815 or Mount Pinatubo in 1991.[1][2] Scientists have emphasized that much of the current understanding of the event depended on two decades of NOAA and NASA investment in stratospheric satellite measurements, and have warned of a potential "stratospheric data desert" if critical instruments are not replaced.[2]

==Footnotes==
The underwater Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcano is located at 20.536°S, 175.382°W, with its summit approximately 150 m below sea level along the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc.[1] The 15 January 2022 eruption has been assigned a volcanic explosivity index (VEI) of approximately 6, placing it among the largest volcanic events ever recorded with modern geophysical instrumentation.[1]

The eruption produced a volcanic plume that reached an altitude of up to 57 km and deposited tephra over an area at least 600 km in diameter, expelling an estimated 2.9 × 10¹⁵ g of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material, including ash and pumice, into the South Pacific Gyre.[1] Following the deposition of ash-laden volcanic plumes, satellite observations revealed elevated surface water chlorophyll-a concentrations in the surrounding ocean.[1]

The likely trigger for the unusually explosive event was a submarine landslide, which removed overlying rock masses and allowed the volcano to explode; emerging island volcanoes are particularly unstable and prone to land-sliding, and powerful eruptions frequently begin with such landslides, as was the case with the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens.[2] Geological evidence also indicates a much larger mid-15th century tsunami in Tonga with a 30-meter run-up height, likely caused by the collapse of an island volcano in the region.[2]

A precursor eruption on 20 December 2021 was followed by another explosive event on 13 January 2022, captured by NOAA's GOES West satellite, which had a radius of 260 km (161.5 miles) and sent ash, steam, and gas 20 km (12.4 miles) into the air—about seven times more powerful than the December 2021 event.[2] A tidal gauge in Nukuʻalofa, the capital of Tonga, measured a 30 cm tsunami wave resulting from that blast.[2] Tsunami waves from the 15 January 2022 eruption were recorded as far as Australia, Japan, and the west coasts of North and South America.[2]

The 2022 eruption has prompted detailed studies of Tongan tsunami deposits, which researchers have emphasized must be undertaken before the deposits are disturbed by human activity or natural events such as tropical storms.[3] Scientists have also called for re-examination of the stratigraphy of many South Pacific stratovolcanoes that have received scant attention, along with new observational and modelling techniques to better understand coupled atmospheric–oceanographic resonance phenomena associated with the event.[3] Dedicated scientific sessions on the eruption were convened by the European Geosciences Union and the Asia Oceania Geosciences Society in May and August 2022, respectively.[3]

Research from the University of Iowa found that, unlike landlocked volcanoes whose particles in the upper atmosphere block sunlight and cause surface cooling, the Hunga Tonga eruption injected unprecedented water vapor 6 to 30 miles above Earth's surface into the stratosphere.[4] Satellite observations showed that the mid-stratosphere cooled by about 7 °F (4 K) for over a year, while the lower stratosphere warmed by approximately 2.7 °F (1–2 K).[4] In the stratosphere, increased water vapor enhances emissions of energy, pushing heat further up into space and back toward Earth while cooling the surrounding atmosphere—behavior opposite to the greenhouse effect observed in the lower atmosphere.[4]

A UCLA-led study found that the sulfate aerosols produced by the eruption were about 50% smaller than those from the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption, making them more effective at blocking sunlight and cooling the atmosphere despite the heavy water vapor load; smaller particles move more erratically and therefore have more opportunities to reflect sunlight.[5] The analysis, which incorporated ozone and other atmospheric components, showed that these factors caused almost instantaneous net radiative energy losses at both the top of the atmosphere and near the tropopause.[5]
